text: This is a case of biliopancreatic diversion with duodenal switch. I found that breaking this procedure into five steps is probably the easiest way of performing it robotically. This is a very pleasant, thirty-four-year-old female who presented initially acutely to the hospital with right-sided heart failure and pulmonary hypertension and a BMI of eighty-four. She underwent cardiac optimization and went through the bariatric process and subsequently underwent a biliopancreatic conversion with a duodenal switch.

The surgical setup includes four 8mm trocars and a 12mm stapling trocar placed in the right mid-clavicular line. A pink star marks the assist port, while a green indicator shows the position of the Nathanson liver retractor.
The surgeon identifies the cecum and runs the ileum back to measure 150 centimeters for creating the common channel. Initially, the measurement is done without markers on the graspers.

The surgeon places clips to anatomically orient the ileum, marking the proximal part with one ten-millimeter clip and the distal part with two clips. The bowel is run, and clips are placed using either a ten or five-millimeter clip applier. The ileum is run another two hundred centimeters, creating a total alimentary limb of three hundred and fifty centimeters. A stitch is placed distally and a single clip proximally to orient the ileum for the duodenal-ileal anastomosis.

The surgeon places a clip and a stitch distally on the bowel loop, which is then pulled to the right and below the eight millimeter trocar located at the right mid-axillary line. This maneuver facilitates the orientation of the bowel for the duodenal ileal anastomosis.
The ileum is tacked to the abdominal wall, and the robotic system is docked in preparation for performing the sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon performs a sleeve gastrectomy using a 36 French bougie, leaving the sleeve slightly more generous than usual. The procedure visually resembles a routine sleeve gastrectomy, with no noticeable increase in reflux compared to standard sleeves in duodenal switches.

The surgeon performs the final firing of the stapler, starting the sleeve gastrectomy with a green load, 60mm stapler, followed by blue loads. The orogastric tube is retracted, and the duodenal division begins. Vessels and peritoneal reflection are taken cephalad to the duodenum, with minimal dissection around the duodenum to preserve its blood supply.

The robotic-assisted procedure is performed using a vessel sealer extend device, with a Ray-Tec sponge placed to manage any minimal bleeding that may occur, ensuring clear visualization of the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects and takes down the duodenal attachments, allowing the duodenum to be lifted and scooped anteriorly under direct visualization, progressing in small increments of one centimeter and five millimeters.

The surgeon places a Penrose drain around the duodenum and uses a 60mm SureForm stapler with a blue load cartridge to complete the division. Indocyanine Green (ICG) is used to identify the common bile duct in challenging cases.

The surgeon places a quarter inch Penrose Drain around the duodenum, facilitating the sliding of staples across it for transection. The staple line is carefully inspected to ensure that only the duodenum is positioned between the anvil and cartridge.

The surgeon achieves excellent hemostasis and aligns the bowel to the duodenum using a mosquito clamp. A double-layer hand-sewn duodenal ileostomy is initiated with a 3-0 Stratafix suture for the posterior staple line. An enterotomy is created, and the inner layer is formed with a 3-0 Vicryl suture. The outer layer is completed with Stratafix monochral, resulting in a wide anastomosis matching the width of the duodenum and the ileum.

After completing the anastomosis, the surgeon creates a window in the mesentery of the biliopancreatic limb and uses a white load, 60mm SureForm stapler to transect it. The roux limb is run backwards to locate clips lining the bowel. A varical suture is used to align the biliopancreatic limb with the common channel or alimentary limb for the creation of a side-to-side, 60mm enteroenterostomy. Scissors are used to create precise enterotomies. The mesenteric defect is closed, followed by a methylene blue and air leak test. The fundus, stomach, and part of the antrum are removed, and the 12mm port site is closed.
